revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:幣帛]]
'''Heihaku''' (幣帛) refers to offerings made to deities in {{ill|Shinto|en|Shinto|qid=Q812767|lt=Shinto|1=Shinto}} rituals, excluding food offerings ({{ill|Shinsen|en|Shinsen|qid=Q11591045|lt=Shinsen|1=Shinsen}}). In a broader sense, it may also include shinsen. It is also known as "mitegura" (御手倉) or "heimotsu" (幣物).
== Overview == The term "帛" (heku) means "cloth," reflecting the importance of textiles, which were considered precious offerings to the deities in ancient times.
Items categorized as heihaku in the {{ill|Engishiki|en|Engishiki|qid=Q1342448|lt=Engishiki|1=Engishiki}} include textiles, clothing, weapons, sake, and food offerings. Since heihaku were both offerings and symbols for divine presence ({{ill|Yorishiro|en|Yorishiro|qid=Q333798|lt=Yorishiro|1=Yorishiro}}), items like gohei (御幣), folded paper streamers ({{ill|Shide (Shinto)|en|Shide (Shinto)|qid=Q2657982|lt=Shide|1=Shide (Shinto)}}), and ritual purification tools like oonusa (大麻) were also called heihaku.
== History == The origins of heihaku can be traced to grave goods excavated from {{ill|Kofun|en|Kofun|qid=Q1141225|lt=Kofun|1=Kofun}}-era Kofuns. Artifacts unearthed from 5th-century sites like the Chisokudai ruins in Chiba Prefecture and the Idutsuku ruins in Ehime Prefecture include iron tools, weaving equipment, textiles, and imported goods like Sue pottery pottery.<ref name="Okada">Okada Shōji, *Nihon Shintōshi*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2010.</ref> These items align with those listed in the heihaku section of the {{ill|Engishiki|en|Engishiki|qid=Q1342448|lt=Engishiki|1=Engishiki}}, forming the prototype of offerings in the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令制度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 律令制}} system.<ref name="Okada" />
Under the ritsuryō system, the {{ill|Department of Divinities|en|Department of Divinities|qid=Q2264720|lt=Department of Divinities|1=Department of Divinities}} distributed heihaku to shrines across Japan during major ceremonies such as the {{ill|Kinen-sai|en|Kinen-sai|qid=Q118906710|lt=Kinensai|1=Kinensai}}, {{ill|Tsukinamisai|ja|月次祭|1=Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinamisai|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|qq=}}, {{ill|Ainosai|ja|相嘗祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ainosai|1=Ainame Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11581944}}, and {{ill|Niiname-no-Matsuri|en|Niiname-no-Matsuri|qid=Q11501518|lt=Niinamesai|1=Niiname-no-Matsuri}}. This practice, known as {{ill|Hanpei|ja|班幣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanpei|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, involved shrine attendants called {{ill|Hōribe|ja|祝部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōribe|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祝 (神職)|1=Hōri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q24869239}} receiving the offerings.<ref name="yougo">{{Cite book|和書|author=National Historical Education Research Council|title=Nihonshi B Yōgo Shū: A Heiki|publisher=Yamakawa Shuppansha|year=2009|isbn=9784634013025}}</ref>
In {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era}} Japan, the "Shinto Ritual Ordinance" (1875) introduced the concept of monetary offerings called "kinpei" (金幣), which were wrapped in paper and presented alongside traditional items like textiles. These offerings were pre-distributed to regional offices for subsequent use in ceremonies.
By January 27, 1947 (Showa 22), the practice of hanpei for {{ill|Kokuhesha|ja|国幣社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokuhesha|1=National Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11420846}} during Kinensai and Niinamesai was halted.<ref>{{Cite book|和書|author=Miyauchi|title=Shōwa Tennō Jitsuroku Volume 10|publisher=Tōkyō Shoseki|year=2017|isbn=9784487744107}}</ref> Today, shrines affiliated with the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|en|Association of Shinto Shrines|qid=Q908077|lt=Association of Shinto Shrines|1=Association of Shinto Shrines}} receive monetary contributions known as "heihakuryō" (幣帛料) for their main festivals.
== Meiji–Taishō Period Heihakuryō == The heihakuryō for shrines during the Taishō period followed regulations such as "Taishō 8 Shikibuchōkan Notification" and "Taishō 9 Ministry of Home Affairs Instruction No. 14." Below are examples:
=== Heihakuryō Amounts for Shrine Ranks === {| class="wikitable" style="text-align:center" |- ! Shrine Rank !! Example Festivals !! Kinensai (per shrine) !! Niinamesai (per shrine) |- | Imperial Large Shrine || 60 yen || 30 yen || 30 yen |- | Imperial Medium Shrine || 50 yen || 25 yen || 25 yen |- | Imperial Small Shrine || 40 yen || 18 yen || 18 yen |- | Prefectural Shrine || 10 yen || 6 yen || 6 yen |- | Village Shrine || 4 yen || 2 yen || 2 yen |}
=== Heihaku Items === Examples of heihaku items for shrines during festivals included textiles, thread, and hemp, as shown below: {| class="wikitable" style="text-align:center" |- ! Shrine Rank !! Textile Length (Shaku) !! Cotton Weight (Kin) !! Hemp Weight (Kin) |- | Imperial Large Shrine || 1.2 || 3 || 3 |- | Imperial Medium Shrine || 1 || 3 || 3 |- | Imperial Small Shrine || 0.8 || 3 || 3 |}
== Heihaku Use and Presentation == Items such as "an" (tables for offerings) and "kome" (sacred straw mats) are used to present heihaku. Heihaku may also be enclosed in "folding chests" (oribitsu) or "karabitsu" for ceremonial purposes.<ref>*Shinto Yūshoku Kojitsu*, Association of Shinto Shrines, 1951.</ref>
== Related Terms == The act of presenting heihaku is called {{ill|Hōbei|ja|奉幣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōbei|1=Hōhei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11445508}}, performed by representatives known as "hōbeishi" (奉幣使) or, in modern times, "heihakukyōshinshi" (幣帛供進使).
== Heihaku in Poetry == The following waka by {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane|2=ja|3=菅原道真|ja|Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane|2=ja|3=菅原道真|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara no Michizane|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q382005}} references heihaku: > このたびは幣もとりあへず手向山 紅葉の錦神のまにまに > (Since I have no heihaku to offer, let the autumn leaves of Mount Tamuke serve as my offering.)
== Notes ==
<references />
== Sources == * {{Cite book|和書|author=National Historical Education Research Council|title=Nihonshi B Yōgo Shū|publisher=Yamakawa Shuppansha|year=2009|isbn=9784634013025}}
{{translated page|ja|幣帛|version=103162368|comment=Imported full ja history}}
[[ja:幣帛]]
<!--Hanpei--><!--Hanpei--> <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{wikidata link|Q11481885}} [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Shinto practices]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Pages with 50+ untranslated japanese characters]]
